items.back: A+ is the generalized inverse giving the minimum-norm least-squares solution x = A+ b for any A (any shape, any rank). Via SVD, A = U S V^T => A+ = V S+ U^T, where S+ inverts the nonzero singular values and transposes. For full-column-rank A it equals (A^T A)^-1 A^T.
items.hint: Invert the nonzero singular values.

items.front: What is the projection matrix onto C(A) for full-column-rank A?
items.back: P = A (A^T A)^-1 A^T. It maps any b to its closest point in the column space: P b = A x_LS. P is symmetric (P^T = P) and idempotent (P^2 = P), the defining properties of an orthogonal projector. (I - P) projects onto the left null space, giving the residual.
items.hint: Symmetric and idempotent.

items.front: Why do practitioners prefer QR or SVD over explicitly forming inv(A^T A)?
items.back: Forming A^T A squares the condition number (cond(A^T A) = cond(A)^2), so a mildly ill-conditioned A becomes badly conditioned and small data errors blow up. QR and SVD work with A directly at condition number cond(A), and never form an explicit inverse, which is both more stable and often faster.
items.hint: Squaring the condition number is the trap.

items.prompt: For an overdetermined full-rank system A x = b, the least-squares solution satisfies:
items.explanation: The normal equations A^T A x = A^T b characterize the least-squares minimizer. A^-1 does not exist for a non-square (tall) A.

items.prompt: Numerically, why avoid solving least squares via x = inv(A^T A) A^T b?
choices: It gives the wrong answer in exact arithmetic
Forming A^T A squares the condition number, amplifying rounding error
It only works for square matrices
items.explanation: The math is correct in exact arithmetic, but cond(A^T A) = cond(A)^2 makes the computation numerically fragile. QR/SVD keep the conditioning at cond(A).

items.prompt: The least-squares residual r = b - A x_LS is:
choices: Parallel to the columns of A
Orthogonal to the column space of A
items.explanation: Minimizing ||b - A x|| forces the residual orthogonal to C(A); that orthogonality (A^T r = 0) is exactly the normal equations.

items.prompt: Which property does an orthogonal projection matrix P satisfy?
items.explanation: Orthogonal projectors are idempotent (P^2 = P) and symmetric (P^T = P). They are generally NOT invertible (they collapse the orthogonal complement to zero).

items.prompt: The pseudoinverse A+ of a tall full-column-rank matrix A equals:
items.explanation: For full column rank, A+ = (A^T A)^-1 A^T (a left inverse: A+ A = I). It produces the least-squares solution x = A+ b.

items.front: Define eigenvalue and eigenvector. What is the characteristic equation?
items.back: For square A, a nonzero vector v with A v = lambda v is an eigenvector; lambda is its eigenvalue. Direction is preserved, only scaled. Eigenvalues are the roots of the characteristic equation det(A - lambda I) = 0; eigenvectors are the nonzero solutions of (A - lambda I) v = 0.
items.hint: A v = lambda v; det(A - lambda I) = 0.

items.front: What does diagonalization A = P D P^-1 mean and when is it possible?
items.back: Columns of P are eigenvectors, D is diagonal of eigenvalues. Possible iff A has n linearly independent eigenvectors (e.g. n distinct eigenvalues, or a symmetric matrix). It decouples the action of A into independent scalings along eigen-directions, making A^k = P D^k P^-1 trivial.
items.hint: Eigenvectors in P, eigenvalues in D.

items.front: What is similarity, and what is invariant under it?
items.back: B is similar to A if B = P^-1 A P for some invertible P; this is just A expressed in a different basis. Eigenvalues, determinant, trace, rank, and characteristic polynomial are all invariant under similarity. Diagonalization is the special case where the new basis is the eigenbasis.
items.hint: Change of basis preserves eigenvalues.

items.front: State the Spectral Theorem for real symmetric (and Hermitian) matrices.
items.back: A real symmetric matrix A (A = A^T) has all real eigenvalues and an ORTHONORMAL set of eigenvectors, so A = Q D Q^T with Q orthogonal. The Hermitian case (A = A^H, conjugate transpose) likewise gives real eigenvalues and orthonormal eigenvectors with A = U D U^H, U unitary. This underpins PCA and array signal processing.
items.hint: Symmetric => real eigenvalues, orthonormal eigenvectors.

items.front: What is a positive-definite matrix and how do eigenvalues characterize it?
items.back: A symmetric matrix A is positive-definite if x^T A x > 0 for all nonzero x, equivalently all eigenvalues are strictly positive. Positive-semidefinite allows >= 0. Covariance matrices are positive-semidefinite. PD matrices have a Cholesky factorization and define valid inner products / quadratic 'energy' forms.
items.hint: All eigenvalues > 0 <=> x^T A x > 0.

items.front: In beamforming / DOA, what do the eigenvectors of the array covariance matrix R reveal?
items.back: R = E[x x^H] for snapshots x. Its eigen-decomposition splits into a signal subspace (eigenvectors of the few large eigenvalues, spanning the directions of arriving sources) and a noise subspace (eigenvectors of the small near-equal eigenvalues). Subspace methods like MUSIC scan steering vectors against the noise subspace; MVDR (Capon) uses R^-1 to place a beam on the look direction while nulling interferers.
items.hint: Large eigenvalues -> signal subspace; small -> noise subspace.

items.front: Why is a Hermitian matrix the right model for an array covariance matrix?
items.back: Array snapshots are complex baseband (I/Q) vectors, so the covariance R = E[x x^H] uses the conjugate transpose and is Hermitian (R = R^H) and positive-semidefinite. The spectral theorem then guarantees real, nonnegative eigenvalues (interpretable as powers) and orthonormal eigenvectors that cleanly separate signal and noise subspaces.
items.hint: Complex I/Q data => conjugate transpose => Hermitian.

items.prompt: If v is an eigenvector of A with eigenvalue lambda, then A v equals:
items.explanation: By definition A v = lambda v: the matrix scales the eigenvector by its eigenvalue without changing its direction.

items.prompt: A real symmetric matrix is guaranteed to have:
items.explanation: The spectral theorem: symmetric matrices have all-real eigenvalues and a full orthonormal set of eigenvectors, giving A = Q D Q^T.

items.prompt: Which quantity is NOT invariant under a similarity transform B = P^-1 A P?
items.explanation: Similarity changes the basis, so the entries change, but eigenvalues, trace, determinant, rank, and characteristic polynomial are preserved.

items.prompt: In the MUSIC algorithm for direction-of-arrival, source directions are found by:
choices: Maximizing the projection of steering vectors onto the noise subspace
Looking for peaks where steering vectors are orthogonal to the noise subspace
Taking the determinant of the covariance matrix
items.explanation: MUSIC exploits that true steering vectors lie in the signal subspace and are therefore orthogonal to the noise subspace; the pseudospectrum 1/||E_noise^H a(theta)||^2 spikes at those directions.

items.prompt: A symmetric matrix with eigenvalues {3, 1, 0} is:
items.explanation: All eigenvalues are >= 0 (so PSD), but one is zero, so it is not strictly positive-definite. A zero eigenvalue also means it is singular.

items.front: State the Singular Value Decomposition and its geometric meaning.
items.back: Any m x n matrix factors as A = U S V^T (V^H if complex), with U (m x m) and V (n x n) orthogonal/unitary and S diagonal with nonnegative singular values sigma1 >= sigma2 >= ... >= 0. Geometrically A is: rotate/reflect by V^T, scale axes by the sigmas, rotate/reflect by U. It exists for every matrix, unlike eigendecomposition.
items.hint: Rotate, scale, rotate.

items.front: How does SVD give the best low-rank approximation of a matrix?
items.back: Eckart-Young: keeping the top k singular triplets, A_k = sum_{i=1}^k sigma_i u_i v_i^T, is the closest rank-k matrix to A in both spectral and Frobenius norm. The discarded sigmas measure the error. This powers compression, denoising, and latent-factor models.
items.hint: Truncate the small singular values.

items.front: Define the condition number via singular values and explain its meaning.
items.back: cond(A) = sigma_max / sigma_min (for the 2-norm). It bounds how much relative input error can be amplified when solving A x = b. A large condition number means an ill-conditioned (nearly singular) problem where solutions are sensitive to noise; cond = 1 is perfectly conditioned (orthogonal matrices).
items.hint: Ratio of largest to smallest singular value.

items.front: How is SVD related to PCA?
items.back: Center the data matrix X (rows = samples). The principal components are the right singular vectors V of X, and the eigenvectors of the covariance X^T X / (n-1); the squared singular values give the variance along each component. So running SVD on centered data is the numerically preferred way to do PCA (no need to form the covariance matrix).
items.hint: Right singular vectors of centered data = principal directions.

items.front: What is Gram-Schmidt and how does it relate to QR decomposition?
items.back: Gram-Schmidt orthogonalizes a set of vectors: subtract from each vector its projections onto the previously found orthonormal vectors, then normalize. Applied to the columns of A it produces A = Q R, where Q has orthonormal columns and R is upper-triangular. (In practice 'modified' Gram-Schmidt or Householder reflections are used for stability.)
items.hint: Orthogonalize columns => Q; the bookkeeping => R.

items.front: Define an inner product, the induced norm, and what orthogonality means.
items.back: An inner product <x,y> (for real vectors x^T y; for complex x^H y) is linear, symmetric/conjugate-symmetric, and positive-definite. It induces the norm ||x|| = sqrt(<x,x>). Two vectors are orthogonal when <x,y> = 0. Orthonormal sets are orthogonal and unit-norm, which makes projections and coordinate computations trivial.
items.hint: Norm = sqrt of inner product with itself.

items.front: Compare the L1, L2, and L-infinity norms of a vector.
items.back: L1 ||x||_1 = sum|x_i| (Manhattan distance; promotes sparsity, e.g. LASSO). L2 ||x||_2 = sqrt(sum x_i^2) (Euclidean length; energy of a signal, used in least squares). L-infinity ||x||_inf = max|x_i| (worst-case / peak magnitude). They coincide only for special vectors; in general L1 >= L2 >= Linf for the same vector's components count.
items.hint: Sum of abs, root-sum-square, max.

items.front: What is a quadratic form and how do its matrix's eigenvalues classify it?
items.back: A quadratic form is q(x) = x^T A x with A symmetric. Diagonalizing A = Q D Q^T turns it into a weighted sum of squares sum lambda_i y_i^2 in eigen-coordinates. Signs of the eigenvalues classify it: all positive => positive-definite (bowl), all negative => negative-definite, mixed => indefinite (saddle).
items.hint: Eigenvalue signs = shape of the bowl.

items.prompt: Which decomposition exists for EVERY m x n matrix?
items.explanation: SVD exists for any matrix of any shape and rank. Eigendecomposition/diagonalization need a square matrix with enough independent eigenvectors; Cholesky needs symmetric positive-definite.

items.prompt: The condition number of an orthogonal matrix Q (in the 2-norm) is:
items.explanation: Orthogonal matrices have all singular values equal to 1, so cond = sigma_max/sigma_min = 1. They preserve length and are perfectly conditioned.

items.prompt: To compress an image matrix with minimal squared error at a given rank k, you should:
choices: Keep the k largest singular values and their singular vectors
Keep the k smallest singular values
Use the eigenvectors of the largest eigenvalues of the image
items.explanation: By the Eckart-Young theorem, the optimal rank-k approximation keeps the k largest singular triplets; the dropped singular values quantify the error.

items.prompt: Which norm tends to produce sparse solutions when used as a regularizer?
items.explanation: The L1 norm's geometry (diamond-shaped level sets with corners on the axes) drives many coefficients exactly to zero, which is why LASSO uses it for sparse feature selection.

items.prompt: If Q has orthonormal columns, then Q^T Q equals:
items.explanation: Orthonormal columns means each pair has dot product 0 and each has unit length, so Q^T Q = I. (Q Q^T = I only when Q is square, i.e. fully orthogonal.)

items.front: What is the general form of a first-order separable ODE, and what is the solution strategy?
items.back: Form: dy/dt = g(t)*h(y). Strategy: separate variables to (1/h(y)) dy = g(t) dt, then integrate both sides. The +C constant is fixed by an initial condition.
items.hint: Get all the y's on one side and all the t's on the other.

items.front: State the standard form of a first-order LINEAR ODE and its integrating factor.
items.back: Standard form: dy/dt + p(t)*y = q(t). Integrating factor: mu(t) = exp(integral of p(t) dt). Multiply through by mu so the left side becomes d/dt[mu*y] = mu*q, then integrate.
items.hint: mu = e raised to the integral of the coefficient of y.

items.front: Define the time constant tau for an RC circuit and for an RL circuit.
items.back: RC: tau = R*C. RL: tau = L/R. tau is the time for the natural (homogeneous) response to decay to 1/e (about 36.8%) of its initial value. After ~5*tau the transient is essentially complete (<1%).
items.hint: One is a product, one is a quotient.

items.front: Write the ODE for the voltage across a capacitor in a series RC circuit driven by a step Vs, and give its solution.
items.back: ODE: R*C*dVc/dt + Vc = Vs. Solution: Vc(t) = Vs + (Vc(0) - Vs)*exp(-t/RC). It is a first-order linear ODE; the forced (particular) part is Vs, the natural part decays with tau = RC.
items.hint: Total response = final value + (initial - final)*exp(-t/tau).

items.front: What is the 'general solution = homogeneous + particular' decomposition, and what does each piece mean physically in a circuit?
items.back: y = y_h + y_p. The homogeneous (natural/transient) solution y_h satisfies the equation with zero forcing and decays via the system's time constants. The particular (forced/steady-state) solution y_p matches the driving input. Total response is their sum.
items.hint: Transient dies out; forced response persists.

items.prompt: A series RL circuit has R = 100 ohm and L = 2 H. What is the time constant tau?
items.explanation: For RL, tau = L/R = 2/100 = 0.02 s (20 ms). (For RC it would be R*C; note RL uses the quotient L/R.)

items.prompt: Solving dy/dt + 3y = 0 with y(0) = 5 gives:
items.explanation: This is separable/linear homogeneous. The solution is y = y(0)*exp(-3t) = 5*exp(-3t); the decay rate is the coefficient of y, here 3, giving tau = 1/3 s.

items.prompt: An RC circuit (tau = RC) is charged toward 10 V from 0 V. Approximately what fraction of the final value is reached after one time constant?
items.explanation: Vc(t) = 10*(1 - exp(-t/tau)). At t = tau, 1 - exp(-1) = 1 - 0.368 = 0.632, i.e. about 63.2% charged. (36.8% is the remaining gap; ~99.3% is reached at 5*tau.)

items.prompt: Which equation is a first-order LINEAR ODE (solvable by an integrating factor)?
items.explanation: Linear means y and dy/dt appear to the first power with no products of y with itself. dy/dt + t*y = sin(t) fits dy/dt + p(t)y = q(t). The y^2, squared derivative, and y*ln(y) terms are nonlinear.

items.front: Why does an inductor current and a capacitor voltage change continuously (not instantaneously) after a switching event?
items.back: Inductor: v = L*di/dt, so an instantaneous (infinite-slope) current change would require infinite voltage. Capacitor: i = C*dv/dt, so an instantaneous voltage change would require infinite current. These continuity conditions set the initial conditions for the transient ODE.
items.hint: Think about what infinite di/dt or dv/dt would demand.

lessons.title: Second-Order Linear ODEs and the RLC System

items.front: Write the standard normalized form of a second-order linear constant-coefficient ODE in terms of omega_n and zeta.
items.back: d2y/dt2 + 2*zeta*omega_n*dy/dt + omega_n^2 * y = f(t). omega_n is the undamped natural frequency, zeta is the damping ratio. The characteristic equation is s^2 + 2*zeta*omega_n*s + omega_n^2 = 0.
items.hint: Coefficient of the first derivative is 2*zeta*omega_n; constant term is omega_n^2.

items.front: Give the characteristic roots of s^2 + 2*zeta*omega_n*s + omega_n^2 = 0 and how zeta selects the damping case.
items.back: s = -zeta*omega_n +/- omega_n*sqrt(zeta^2 - 1). zeta > 1: two real roots = overdamped (no ring). zeta = 1: repeated real root = critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory). zeta < 1: complex conjugate roots = underdamped (rings). zeta = 0: pure imaginary = undamped oscillation.
items.hint: The discriminant zeta^2 - 1 controls whether roots are real or complex.

items.front: For a SERIES RLC circuit, give omega_n, zeta, and the damped ringing frequency omega_d.
items.back: omega_n = 1/sqrt(L*C). zeta = (R/2)*sqrt(C/L) (equivalently alpha/omega_n with alpha = R/(2L)). Damped frequency omega_d = omega_n*sqrt(1 - zeta^2), valid when underdamped (zeta < 1).
items.hint: Natural frequency depends only on L and C; R sets the damping.

items.front: How do you decide, from component values, whether a series RLC circuit RINGS (oscillates) in its transient?
items.back: Compute zeta = (R/2)*sqrt(C/L) (or compare R to the critical value 2*sqrt(L/C)). If zeta < 1 (R < 2*sqrt(L/C)) it is underdamped and rings at omega_d. If zeta >= 1 (R >= 2*sqrt(L/C)) it is critically or overdamped and does not oscillate.
items.hint: Ringing requires complex roots, i.e. zeta < 1.

items.front: Define the quality factor Q of an RLC resonator and relate it to zeta.
items.back: Q = 1/(2*zeta). For a series RLC, Q = (1/R)*sqrt(L/C); for parallel RLC, Q = R*sqrt(C/L). High Q means low damping (sharp resonance, long ringdown, narrow bandwidth). Bandwidth = omega_n/Q.
items.hint: Q is inversely proportional to zeta.

items.prompt: A series RLC has L = 1 mH, C = 1 uF, R = 10 ohm. The critical resistance is 2*sqrt(L/C) = 2*sqrt(1000) ~= 63.2 ohm. The circuit is:
items.explanation: R = 10 ohm is well below the critical 63.2 ohm, so zeta < 1. The roots are complex, the transient rings at omega_d, and the circuit is underdamped.

items.prompt: Which damping case gives the FASTEST return to steady state with NO overshoot/oscillation?
items.explanation: Critical damping (zeta = 1, repeated root) is the boundary that settles fastest without oscillating. Underdamped overshoots/rings; overdamped is non-oscillatory but slower; undamped oscillates forever.

items.prompt: For an LC tank with L = 100 mH and C = 100 nF (no resistance), the natural frequency omega_n = 1/sqrt(LC) is approximately:
items.explanation: LC = 0.1 * 100e-9 = 1e-8, sqrt(LC) = 1e-4, so omega_n = 1/1e-4 = 1e4 = 10000 rad/s (about 1.59 kHz).

items.prompt: An underdamped second-order system has roots s = -2 +/- j5. What are its decay rate (alpha) and ringing frequency (omega_d)?
items.explanation: For complex roots s = -alpha +/- j*omega_d, the real part magnitude is the decay rate alpha = 2 and the imaginary part is the damped ringing frequency omega_d = 5 rad/s.

items.prompt: A high-Q series RLC resonator (small R) will, in its step/impulse transient, behave how?
choices: Ring for many cycles before settling
items.explanation: High Q means low zeta (Q = 1/(2*zeta)), so the system is lightly damped: it rings for roughly Q cycles before the envelope decays. Resonance is sharp and the ringdown is long.

items.front: How does the impulse response of a second-order system relate to its step response?
items.back: The impulse response h(t) is the time derivative of the step response (and the step response is the integral of the impulse response). For an underdamped RLC, h(t) is a decaying sinusoid exp(-alpha*t)*sin(omega_d*t)-type term; the step response is that plus the final settled value.
items.hint: Differentiate the step to get the impulse response.

items.front: Why can sinusoidal steady-state circuit analysis replace solving the differential equation with algebra (phasors)?
items.back: For a linear time-invariant ODE driven by A*cos(omega*t), the particular solution is also a sinusoid at omega. Representing signals as complex phasors and using d/dt -> j*omega turns the ODE into an algebraic equation. Impedances (Z_R=R, Z_L=j*omega*L, Z_C=1/(j*omega*C)) come directly from this substitution.
items.hint: Each time derivative becomes multiplication by j*omega.

items.front: What is the transfer function H(s), and how do you read frequency response and stability from it?
items.back: H(s) is the Laplace-domain ratio output/input of a linear system, H(s) = N(s)/D(s). The roots of D(s) are the poles (= characteristic roots = system natural frequencies); roots of N(s) are zeros. Frequency response is H(j*omega). Stability requires all poles in the left half-plane (negative real part).
items.hint: Poles come from the denominator = characteristic equation.

items.front: Write the general state-space form for a linear system and name the matrices.
items.back: dx/dt = A*x + B*u, y = C*x + D*u. x is the state vector, u the input, y the output. A is the state (system) matrix, B the input matrix, C the output matrix, D the feedthrough. A first-order vector ODE; any higher-order ODE can be rewritten this way.
items.hint: x-dot equals A x plus B u.

items.front: What is the key relationship between the eigenvalues of the state matrix A and the system's dynamics?
items.back: The eigenvalues of A ARE the system's natural frequencies / poles (roots of det(sI - A) = 0, the characteristic equation). Their real parts set decay/growth, imaginary parts set oscillation frequency. Negative real parts => stable; complex pairs => ringing.
items.hint: det(sI - A) = 0 is the characteristic equation.

items.front: How do you convert a second-order ODE y'' + a*y' + b*y = u into state-space form?
items.back: Pick states x1 = y, x2 = y'. Then x1' = x2 and x2' = -b*x1 - a*x2 + u. So A = [[0, 1], [-b, -a]], B = [[0], [1]], C = [1, 0], D = 0. The eigenvalues of A reproduce the characteristic roots of the original ODE.
items.hint: Let the states be the function and its derivative.

items.prompt: In phasor analysis, the time-domain operation d/dt corresponds to which algebraic operation?
items.explanation: Differentiating e^(j*omega*t) gives j*omega*e^(j*omega*t). So d/dt becomes multiplication by j*omega, which is exactly why inductor impedance is j*omega*L and capacitor impedance is 1/(j*omega*C) (integration is division by j*omega).

items.prompt: A linear system has state matrix A = [[0, 1], [-4, -0]] (i.e. y'' + 4y = 0). Its eigenvalues are:
items.explanation: det(sI - A) = s^2 + 4 = 0, so s = +/-j2. Purely imaginary eigenvalues mean an undamped oscillator at omega = 2 rad/s (the LC-tank case, zeta = 0).

items.prompt: A continuous-time linear system is asymptotically STABLE if and only if all eigenvalues of A (poles) have:
items.explanation: Continuous-time stability requires every pole/eigenvalue in the open left half-plane (negative real part) so each mode exp(s*t) decays. (Magnitude < 1 is the discrete-time/z-domain criterion.)

items.prompt: At the resonant frequency of a series RLC driven sinusoidally, the inductive and capacitive reactances:
items.explanation: At omega = omega_n = 1/sqrt(LC), j*omega*L and 1/(j*omega*C) are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign, so they cancel. The series impedance reduces to R (a minimum), giving maximum current at resonance.

items.prompt: Putting a system in state-space form dx/dt = A*x + B*u is most directly motivated by which benefit?
choices: It eliminates the need for initial conditions
It reduces any order-n linear ODE to a first-order vector ODE amenable to matrix/eigenvalue and numerical methods
items.explanation: State-space recasts an nth-order ODE as a single first-order vector ODE. This enables eigenvalue analysis (modes/poles), controllability/observability, and standard numerical integration (Euler, Runge-Kutta) which expect dx/dt = f(x,u).

items.front: Write the 1-D wave equation and the 1-D heat/diffusion equation, and contrast their behavior.
items.back: Wave: d2u/dt2 = c^2 * d2u/dx2 (second order in time; supports propagating/standing waves, energy-conserving). Heat/diffusion: du/dt = k * d2u/dx2 (first order in time; smooths/dissipates, irreversible). c is wave speed, k is diffusivity.
items.hint: Wave is second-order in time; heat is first-order in time.

items.front: Explain separation of variables for the wave equation and what 'modes' result.
items.back: Assume u(x,t) = X(x)*T(t). Substituting splits the PDE into two ODEs sharing a separation constant. Boundary conditions quantize X(x) into discrete spatial eigenfunctions (e.g. sin(n*pi*x/L)) = standing-wave modes; each has its own time oscillation T_n(t). The full solution is a superposition (Fourier series) of these modes.
items.hint: u = X(x)*T(t) turns one PDE into two ODEs plus an eigenvalue problem.

items.front: How do boundary conditions on a vibrating string of length L produce a discrete set of resonant frequencies?
items.back: Fixed ends require X(0)=X(L)=0, which forces X_n(x) = sin(n*pi*x/L) for n = 1,2,3,... The allowed (quantized) wavenumbers give standing-wave modes with frequencies omega_n = (n*pi*c)/L (fundamental plus harmonics). Boundary conditions select which modes can exist.
items.hint: Only sinusoids that vanish at both ends survive.

items.front: How does the wave equation connect to electromagnetics?
items.back: Maxwell's equations in free space combine to give wave equations for E and B: d2E/dt2 = c^2 * laplacian(E), with c = 1/sqrt(mu0*eps0). EM fields propagate as waves at speed c; the same separation-of-variables/standing-wave analysis yields waveguide and cavity-resonator modes used in radar.
items.hint: Maxwell's equations reduce to wave equations with speed 1/sqrt(mu0*eps0).

items.front: State the forward Euler method for dy/dt = f(t,y) and its main weakness.
items.back: Euler: y_{n+1} = y_n + h * f(t_n, y_n), with step size h. It is first-order accurate (local error ~ h^2, global ~ h). Weakness: low accuracy and poor stability; small h needed, and it can blow up on stiff or oscillatory problems unless h is tiny.
items.hint: Take one slope at the current point and step forward.

items.front: What does classical 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) do better than Euler, and at what cost?
items.back: RK4 evaluates the slope f four times per step (k1 at start, k2/k3 at midpoint, k4 at end) and takes a weighted average, giving 4th-order global accuracy (error ~ h^4). Far more accurate/stable per step than Euler; cost is 4 function evaluations per step.
items.hint: Four slope samples (k1..k4) weighted 1-2-2-1 over 6.

items.prompt: Which PDE is first-order in time and models irreversible smoothing/dissipation (e.g. temperature spreading)?
items.explanation: The heat/diffusion equation du/dt = k*u_xx is first-order in time and dissipative: sharp gradients smooth out and the process is irreversible, unlike the energy-conserving (second-order-in-time) wave equation.

items.prompt: Separation of variables u(x,t) = X(x)*T(t) on a PDE works because:
choices: Each side of the rearranged equation depends on a different variable, so both must equal a constant
It converts the PDE to an algebraic equation directly
items.explanation: After substituting and dividing, one side depends only on x and the other only on t; since they are equal for all x and t, both equal a common separation constant. This yields two ODEs plus an eigenvalue problem set by the boundary conditions.

items.prompt: A string fixed at both ends (length L) has spatial modes sin(n*pi*x/L). The fundamental (n=1) and its overtones are called:
items.explanation: The quantized solutions sin(n*pi*x/L) are the standing-wave modes; n=1 is the fundamental and higher n are harmonics. The boundary conditions select this discrete set, analogous to resonant modes in cavities/waveguides.

items.prompt: Halving the step size h in the forward Euler method (a first-order method) roughly changes the global error by what factor?
items.explanation: Euler has global error proportional to h (first-order). Halving h roughly halves the global error. RK4, being 4th-order, would cut error by about a factor of 16 for the same halving.

items.prompt: Why might forward Euler 'blow up' on a lightly damped (high-Q) RLC simulation while RK4 stays stable at the same h?
choices: Euler's stability region is small, so oscillatory poles near the imaginary axis amplify each step unless h is tiny
items.explanation: Forward Euler has a small stability region; for poles near the imaginary axis (lightly damped oscillation) it adds energy each step and diverges unless h is very small. RK4's larger stability region handles such oscillatory dynamics better at the same step size.

items.front: What is the core claim of the Fourier series?
items.back: Any reasonable periodic signal x(t) with period T can be written as a sum of sinusoids whose frequencies are integer multiples (harmonics) of the fundamental frequency f0 = 1/T. The signal is fully described by the amplitudes and phases of those harmonics.
items.hint: Periodic signals = sum of harmonically related sinusoids.

items.front: Write the trigonometric form of the Fourier series and name each term.
items.back: x(t) = a0 + sum_{n=1..inf} [ a_n*cos(2*pi*n*f0*t) + b_n*sin(2*pi*n*f0*t) ]. a0 is the DC (average) term, a_n and b_n are the cosine and sine harmonic coefficients, and n*f0 is the n-th harmonic frequency.
items.hint: DC term plus cosine and sine sums over harmonics.

items.front: Write the complex-exponential (exponential) form of the Fourier series.
items.back: x(t) = sum_{n=-inf..inf} c_n * e^(j*2*pi*n*f0*t), where c_n = (1/T) * integral_over_T x(t)*e^(-j*2*pi*n*f0*t) dt. The c_n are complex; |c_n| gives amplitude and angle(c_n) gives phase of harmonic n. Negative-n terms are mathematical conjugate partners of positive ones for real signals.
items.hint: Sum of c_n times e^(j*2*pi*n*f0*t).

items.front: State Parseval's theorem for the Fourier series and its engineering meaning.
items.back: (1/T)*integral_over_T |x(t)|^2 dt = sum_{n=-inf..inf} |c_n|^2. The average power of a periodic signal equals the sum of the powers of its individual harmonics. Power is conserved between time and frequency domains; the spectrum tells you how power is distributed across harmonics.
items.hint: Average power = sum of |c_n|^2; power conservation.

items.front: What is the Gibbs phenomenon?
items.back: When a Fourier series is truncated to a finite number of harmonics to approximate a signal with a jump discontinuity (e.g. a square wave), the partial sum overshoots near the jump by about 9 percent of the jump height. Adding more harmonics narrows the overshoot but does not reduce its amplitude; it never disappears.
items.hint: ~9% overshoot at discontinuities that never goes away.

items.front: What does the 'spectrum' of a periodic signal look like, and why is it discrete?
items.back: It is a line spectrum: discrete spikes located only at integer-multiple frequencies n*f0 (DC, fundamental, harmonics), with each spike's height set by |c_n| (magnitude spectrum) and a matching phase spectrum from angle(c_n). It is discrete because a periodic signal contains only harmonically related frequencies, nothing in between.
items.hint: Discrete lines at n*f0; periodicity forces a line spectrum.

items.prompt: A signal is periodic with period T = 2 ms. What is the frequency of its 3rd harmonic?
items.explanation: Fundamental f0 = 1/T = 1/0.002 = 500 Hz. The n-th harmonic is at n*f0, so the 3rd harmonic is 3*500 = 1500 Hz.

items.prompt: An ideal square wave (odd symmetry, 50% duty cycle) contains which harmonics?
items.explanation: A symmetric square wave has half-wave (odd) symmetry, so all even harmonics vanish and only odd harmonics (1, 3, 5, ...) are present, with amplitudes falling off as 1/n.

items.prompt: For a real-valued periodic signal, the magnitude spectrum |c_n| is:
choices: An even function of n (symmetric about n=0)
items.explanation: Real signals have Hermitian symmetry c_(-n) = conjugate(c_n), so |c_(-n)| = |c_n|. The magnitude spectrum is even (symmetric); the phase spectrum is odd.

lessons.title: Continuous Fourier Transform, Convolution, and LTI Systems

items.front: State the continuous-time Fourier transform (CTFT) and its inverse.
items.back: Forward: X(f) = integral_{-inf..inf} x(t)*e^(-j*2*pi*f*t) dt. Inverse: x(t) = integral_{-inf..inf} X(f)*e^(+j*2*pi*f*t) df. The CTFT generalizes the Fourier series to aperiodic signals: the discrete line spectrum becomes a continuous spectral density X(f).
items.hint: X(f) = integral of x(t)*e^(-j*2*pi*f*t) dt.

items.front: How does the Fourier transform relate to the Fourier series conceptually?
items.back: Take a periodic signal and let its period T go to infinity. The harmonics n*f0 get closer together (spacing f0 = 1/T shrinks), the line spectrum fills in, and the sum becomes an integral. The Fourier series describes periodic signals (discrete spectrum); the Fourier transform describes aperiodic signals (continuous spectrum).
items.hint: Let T -> infinity; lines merge into a continuum.

items.front: List the key Fourier transform property pairs (time/freq shift, scaling, convolution, modulation, duality).
items.back: Linearity: a*x+b*y <-> a*X+b*Y. Time shift: x(t-t0) <-> X(f)*e^(-j*2*pi*f*t0) (phase ramp, magnitude unchanged). Freq shift/modulation: x(t)*e^(j*2*pi*fc*t) <-> X(f-fc). Scaling: x(a*t) <-> (1/|a|)*X(f/a) (compress in time = stretch in freq). Convolution: x*h <-> X*H. Multiplication: x*y <-> X (convolve) Y. Duality: if x(t)<->X(f) then X(t)<->x(-f).
items.hint: Shift=phase, scale inverts width, convolution<->multiplication.

items.front: Give the essential CTFT pairs every DSP engineer should know.
items.back: Delta: delta(t) <-> 1 (flat spectrum). Constant 1 <-> delta(f). Rectangular pulse rect(t/T) <-> T*sinc(f*T). Sinc <-> rect (by duality). Gaussian <-> Gaussian. cos(2*pi*fc*t) <-> (1/2)[delta(f-fc)+delta(f+fc)]. Two-sided exponential and e^(-a*t)*u(t) <-> 1/(a + j*2*pi*f).
items.hint: Delta<->1, rect<->sinc, cos<->two impulses.

items.front: State the convolution theorem and why it matters for radar and CNNs.
items.back: Convolution in time equals multiplication in frequency: y(t) = x(t) convolved with h(t)  <->  Y(f) = X(f)*H(f). This underpins LTI filtering, the matched filter in radar (correlation = convolution with a time-reversed replica, maximizing SNR against noise), and fast/FFT-based convolution used to accelerate convolutional layers in CNNs.
items.hint: Conv in time = multiply in freq; matched filter and fast conv.

items.front: What defines an LTI system, and how is it characterized in time and frequency?
items.back: Linear Time-Invariant: it obeys superposition (linearity) and a time-shifted input produces the same output shifted in time. An LTI system is fully characterized by its impulse response h(t): output y(t) = x(t) convolved with h(t). In frequency the same system is the frequency response H(f) = FT{h(t)}, which scales and phase-shifts each input frequency.
items.hint: Impulse response h(t); convolution; H(f) is its transform.

items.front: What is the impulse response and why is it so powerful?
items.back: h(t) is the output of an LTI system when the input is a Dirac delta impulse. Because any input can be expressed as a continuum of weighted, shifted impulses, knowing h(t) lets you compute the response to ANY input via convolution. It is the complete time-domain fingerprint of the system.
items.hint: Output to a delta; convolve with it to get any response.

items.prompt: Compressing a pulse in time by a factor of 2 (making it half as wide) does what to its Fourier spectrum?
choices: Stretches the spectrum by 2 (it gets wider)
Shifts the spectrum up by 2 Hz
items.explanation: By the scaling property x(a*t) <-> (1/|a|)X(f/a), compressing in time stretches the spectrum. Narrow pulses need wide bandwidth, the time-bandwidth tradeoff exploited by radar pulse compression.

items.prompt: What is the Fourier transform of a Dirac delta impulse, delta(t)?
choices: A constant value of 1 for all f
items.explanation: FT{delta(t)} = 1 for all frequencies: an impulse contains every frequency equally. This is why probing an LTI system with an impulse reveals its full frequency response.

items.prompt: Delaying a signal by t0 seconds affects its Fourier transform how?
choices: Multiplies X(f) by e^(-j*2*pi*f*t0), changing phase but not magnitude
Shifts the spectrum to X(f - t0)
items.explanation: Time shift adds a linear phase term: x(t - t0) <-> X(f)*e^(-j*2*pi*f*t0). The magnitude spectrum is unchanged; only the phase gets a frequency-proportional ramp.

items.prompt: A radar matched filter correlates the received echo with a replica of the transmitted pulse. In Fourier terms, why does this maximize SNR?
choices: Its frequency response is the conjugate of the signal spectrum, coherently summing signal energy while noise adds incoherently
It is a simple low-pass filter
items.explanation: The matched filter H(f) = conjugate(X(f)) (times a delay) aligns the phases of all signal frequency components so they add coherently at the output peak, while white noise adds incoherently, giving the maximum achievable output SNR for a known signal in white noise. Note: multiplication in time = convolution in frequency, which is also why windowing smears a spectrum.

lessons.title: Laplace Transform, Transfer Functions, Poles and Zeros

items.front: Define the (one-sided) Laplace transform and the complex variable s.
items.back: X(s) = integral_{0..inf} x(t)*e^(-s*t) dt, where s = sigma + j*omega is complex. The real part sigma is an exponential growth/decay rate; the imaginary part omega is angular frequency. The Laplace transform generalizes the Fourier transform by including a real damping factor e^(-sigma*t), letting it handle growing/transient signals the FT cannot.
items.hint: Integral of x(t)*e^(-s*t); s = sigma + j*omega.

items.front: How does the Laplace transform relate to the Fourier transform?
items.back: Set sigma = 0, i.e. evaluate the Laplace transform on the imaginary axis s = j*omega (with omega = 2*pi*f), and you recover the Fourier transform, provided the imaginary axis lies inside the region of convergence. Laplace = Fourier with an added e^(-sigma*t) convergence factor.
items.hint: Laplace on s = j*omega gives the Fourier transform.

items.front: What is the Region of Convergence (ROC) and why does it matter?
items.back: The ROC is the set of s values (a range of sigma) for which the Laplace integral converges to a finite value. Different time signals can share the same algebraic X(s) but differ in ROC, so the ROC is required to make the transform unique and to determine causality and stability. For a causal system, the ROC is a right-half plane to the right of the rightmost pole.
items.hint: Where the integral converges; needed for uniqueness and stability.

items.front: List essential Laplace transform pairs.
items.hint: step=1/s, exp=1/(s+a), cos=s/(s^2+w0^2).

items.front: State the key Laplace properties used to solve ODEs and RLC circuits.
items.back: Linearity. Time-domain differentiation: L{x'(t)} = s*X(s) - x(0). Second derivative: s^2*X(s) - s*x(0) - x'(0). Integration: L{integral x} = X(s)/s. Time shift: x(t-a)*u(t-a) <-> e^(-a*s)*X(s). Convolution: x*h <-> X(s)*H(s). Differentiation turns calculus ODEs into algebra, and s=1/(s*C) and s*L give impedances for capacitors and inductors.
items.hint: d/dt -> s (minus initial conditions); convolution -> product.

items.front: Define a transfer function H(s) and how to read off poles and zeros.
items.back: H(s) = Y(s)/X(s) = the Laplace transform of the impulse response, written as a ratio of polynomials N(s)/D(s). Zeros are the roots of the numerator N(s) (where H=0); poles are the roots of the denominator D(s) (where H blows up). Poles set the natural/resonant modes and stability; zeros shape the response by suppressing certain frequencies.
items.hint: H(s)=N(s)/D(s); zeros=numerator roots, poles=denominator roots.

items.front: State the stability rule for a continuous-time LTI system in the s-plane.
items.back: A causal LTI system is BIBO-stable if and only if ALL poles of H(s) lie strictly in the left half-plane (Re{s} < 0). Poles on the imaginary axis give marginal stability/oscillation; any pole in the right half-plane (Re{s} > 0) causes an exponentially growing, unstable response.
items.hint: All poles in the left half-plane = stable.

items.prompt: A continuous-time LTI system has poles at s = -2 and s = +1. Is it stable?
choices: Stable, because it has a pole at -2
Unstable, because the pole at +1 is in the right half-plane
Stable, because the poles average to a negative value
items.explanation: Stability requires ALL poles to have Re{s} < 0. The pole at s = +1 lies in the right half-plane, producing a term that grows like e^(+t), so the system is unstable regardless of the other pole.

items.prompt: In the Laplace domain, the impedance of an inductor L and a capacitor C are:
items.explanation: Inductor voltage v = L*di/dt gives impedance Z_L = s*L; capacitor current i = C*dv/dt gives Z_C = 1/(s*C). These let you analyze RLC circuits with algebra just like resistors.

items.prompt: Why is the region of convergence (ROC) necessary in addition to the algebraic expression X(s)?
choices: It scales the amplitude of X(s)
Different time signals can yield the same X(s) formula but different ROCs, so ROC makes the transform unique and indicates causality/stability
It is only relevant for periodic signals
items.explanation: A right-sided causal exponential and a left-sided anti-causal one can share the same X(s) algebra but have different ROCs. The ROC resolves the ambiguity and tells you whether the imaginary axis (and thus the Fourier transform) and stability conditions are satisfied.

lessons.title: Discrete-Time: DTFT, DFT, and the FFT

items.front: What is the DTFT (discrete-time Fourier transform) and how does it differ from the DFT?
items.back: DTFT: X(e^(j*w)) = sum_{n=-inf..inf} x[n]*e^(-j*w*n). It takes a discrete-time sequence (possibly infinite) and produces a CONTINUOUS, 2*pi-periodic spectrum. The DFT samples this continuous DTFT at N equally spaced frequencies, producing a finite, computable spectrum for an N-point sequence.
items.hint: DTFT: continuous, periodic spectrum from discrete samples.

items.front: Define the N-point DFT and its inverse.
items.back: Forward: X[k] = sum_{n=0..N-1} x[n]*e^(-j*2*pi*k*n/N), for k = 0..N-1. Inverse: x[n] = (1/N)*sum_{k=0..N-1} X[k]*e^(+j*2*pi*k*n/N). It maps N time samples to N complex frequency-bin values. Bin k corresponds to frequency k*fs/N.
items.hint: X[k] = sum x[n]*e^(-j*2*pi*k*n/N).

items.front: What is the FFT and why does it matter?
items.back: The FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) is an efficient algorithm (e.g. Cooley-Tukey, divide-and-conquer) that computes the exact same DFT result but in O(N*log N) operations instead of the direct DFT's O(N^2). For N = 1024 that is roughly a 100x speedup; for large N it makes real-time spectral analysis, fast convolution, and radar Doppler processing practical.
items.hint: Fast algorithm for the DFT: O(N log N) vs O(N^2).

items.front: What is the DFT bin spacing (frequency resolution) and how do you find the frequency of bin k?
items.back: Bin spacing = fs/N, where fs is the sample rate and N is the number of points. Bin k sits at frequency f_k = k*fs/N for k = 0..N/2, and bins above N/2 represent negative frequencies. To improve resolution (smaller fs/N) you must increase N by acquiring a longer record of real data.
items.hint: Bin spacing = fs/N; f_k = k*fs/N.

items.front: What is spectral leakage and what causes it?
items.back: Leakage is the spreading of a tone's energy across many DFT bins instead of a single bin. It happens because the DFT assumes the N-sample record repeats periodically; if the signal does not complete an integer number of cycles in the window, the implied periodic extension has discontinuities, whose sharp edges contain extra frequencies. Equivalently, the rectangular window's sinc-shaped spectrum convolves with the true line spectrum.
items.hint: Non-integer cycles in the window -> energy smears across bins.

items.front: What is windowing and what tradeoff does it impose?
items.back: Windowing multiplies the time record by a smooth taper (Hann, Hamming, Blackman, Kaiser, ...) that goes to zero at the edges, removing the discontinuity and reducing leakage/sidelobes. The tradeoff: tapering widens the main lobe, so you trade frequency resolution (main-lobe width) for lower sidelobes (less leakage). Rectangular = narrowest main lobe but worst leakage.
items.hint: Taper edges to cut leakage; costs main-lobe width.

items.front: Which libraries provide FFTs and what are their roles?
items.back: NumPy: numpy.fft (np.fft.fft, rfft for real inputs) for general-purpose work. SciPy: scipy.fft / scipy.signal, often faster and with more tools (windows via scipy.signal.windows, spectrograms). FFTW ('Fastest Fourier Transform in the West'): a highly optimized C library that auto-tunes a 'plan' for the hardware; pyfftw wraps it. MATLAB's fft, and cuFFT for GPUs.
items.hint: numpy.fft, scipy.fft, FFTW (planning, C library).

items.prompt: You take a 1024-point FFT of data sampled at fs = 48 kHz. What is the frequency spacing between adjacent bins?
items.explanation: Bin spacing = fs/N = 48000/1024 = 46.875 Hz. Each bin k is centered at k*46.875 Hz.

items.prompt: Roughly how many complex multiply-adds does a radix-2 FFT need for N = 4096 compared to the direct DFT?
choices: FFT ~ N*log2(N) vs DFT ~ N^2, roughly a 340x reduction
FFT is slower than the direct DFT
items.explanation: Direct DFT is O(N^2) = ~16.8 million; FFT is O(N*log2 N) = 4096*12 = ~49,000. The ratio N/log2(N) = 4096/12 is about 340x fewer operations, which is why the FFT made real-time spectral processing feasible.

items.prompt: A pure sinusoid whose frequency falls exactly halfway between two DFT bins will:
choices: Appear as a single clean spike
Leak energy into many bins (scalloping loss and leakage)
Not appear in the spectrum at all
items.explanation: Off-bin tones do not complete an integer number of cycles in the record, so their energy leaks across bins and the peak is attenuated (scalloping loss). Windowing reduces the leakage; zero-padding helps locate the true peak.

items.prompt: Applying a Hann window before the FFT versus using no window (rectangular) primarily:
choices: Improves frequency resolution and lowers leakage simultaneously
Lowers sidelobe leakage at the cost of a wider main lobe (worse resolution)
Has no measurable effect on the spectrum
items.explanation: A Hann window suppresses the high sidelobes that cause leakage, but its smooth taper broadens the main lobe, so closely spaced tones are harder to resolve. It is a resolution-versus-leakage tradeoff. (Zero-padding, by contrast, only interpolates the bin grid and adds no true resolution.)

lessons.title: z-Transform, Digital Filters, and the Big Picture

items.front: Define the z-transform and its complex variable z.
items.back: X(z) = sum_{n=-inf..inf} x[n]*z^(-n), where z is complex, often written z = r*e^(j*w). It is the discrete-time counterpart of the Laplace transform. Each unit delay in time corresponds to multiplication by z^(-1), which is why z^(-1) is drawn as a delay block in filter diagrams.
items.hint: X(z) = sum x[n]*z^(-n); z^(-1) = one-sample delay.

items.front: What is the ROC of the z-transform and what shape does it take?
items.back: The ROC is the set of z values for which the sum converges; it is always an annulus (ring) in the z-plane bounded by poles. For a causal (right-sided) sequence the ROC is the exterior of a circle through the outermost pole (|z| > r_max). The ROC must include the unit circle for the DTFT to exist and for the system to be stable.
items.hint: A ring bounded by poles; causal = outside outermost pole.

items.front: Describe the s-plane to z-plane mapping and the stability correspondence.
items.back: The exact map is z = e^(s*T) (T = sampling period). The s-plane imaginary axis maps to the z-plane unit circle; the entire left half-plane (stable region in s) maps to the INTERIOR of the unit circle; the right half-plane maps outside. So 'all poles left of j*omega axis' (continuous stability) becomes 'all poles inside the unit circle' (discrete stability).
items.hint: z = e^(s*T): LHP -> inside unit circle; j-axis -> unit circle.

items.front: State the stability condition for a causal discrete-time LTI system in the z-plane.
items.back: A causal discrete LTI system is BIBO-stable if and only if ALL poles of H(z) lie strictly INSIDE the unit circle (|pole| < 1). Poles on the unit circle give marginal stability/oscillation; any pole with |z| > 1 makes the system unstable (response grows without bound).
items.hint: All poles inside the unit circle = stable.

items.front: What is a digital filter transfer function H(z), and what distinguishes FIR from IIR?
items.back: H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) = (b0 + b1*z^(-1) + ... + bM*z^(-M)) / (a0 + a1*z^(-1) + ... + aN*z^(-N)). FIR filters have only numerator (feed-forward) terms (no feedback poles except at z=0), are always stable, and can be exactly linear-phase. IIR filters have a denominator (feedback/poles), achieve sharp responses with fewer taps, but can be unstable if poles leave the unit circle.
items.hint: Ratio of z^(-1) polynomials; FIR=no feedback, IIR=has poles.

items.front: Give the big-picture map relating CTFT, Laplace, DTFT, DFT, and z-transform.
items.back: Continuous time: Fourier transform (CTFT) handles aperiodic analog signals; Laplace generalizes it with s = sigma + j*omega (j*omega axis = Fourier). Discrete time: DTFT handles sampled signals (continuous, periodic spectrum); z-transform generalizes it with z = r*e^(j*w) (unit circle = DTFT). The DFT/FFT is the finite, computable, sampled version of the DTFT. Stability: LHP for s, interior of unit circle for z, linked by z = e^(s*T).
items.hint: Laplace:Fourier :: z:DTFT; DFT = sampled DTFT.

items.front: Modern time-frequency tools: STFT/spectrograms and wavelets.
items.back: STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform) slides a window along the signal and takes an FFT of each segment, giving a time-frequency map; its magnitude squared is the spectrogram. It has a FIXED time-frequency resolution tradeoff (Heisenberg/Gabor limit set by window length). Wavelets use scaled, shifted basis functions giving MULTI-RESOLUTION analysis: good time resolution at high frequencies and good frequency resolution at low frequencies, better for transients.
items.hint: STFT = windowed FFTs (fixed resolution); wavelets = multi-resolution.

items.prompt: A digital filter has poles at z = 0.8 and z = 1.2. Is the causal system stable?
choices: Unstable, the pole at z = 1.2 is outside the unit circle
Stable, the average magnitude is below 1
items.explanation: Discrete-time stability requires ALL poles to satisfy |z| < 1. The pole at z = 1.2 lies outside the unit circle, producing a 1.2^n term that grows without bound, so the system is unstable.

items.prompt: Under the mapping z = e^(s*T), the stable region of the s-plane (left half-plane) maps to which region of the z-plane?
choices: The interior of the unit circle (|z| < 1)
The right half of the z-plane
items.explanation: With z = e^(s*T), Re{s} < 0 implies |z| = e^(sigma*T) < 1. The left half-plane maps to the interior of the unit circle, the j*omega axis maps to the unit circle, and the right half-plane maps outside.

items.prompt: In a digital filter block diagram, the element labeled z^(-1) represents:
items.explanation: Multiplication by z^(-1) in the z-domain corresponds to a delay of one sample in time: x[n-1] <-> z^(-1)*X(z). It is drawn as a unit-delay (register) block.

items.prompt: What fundamentally limits the joint time-frequency resolution of a spectrogram (STFT)?
choices: The fixed window length: a short window gives good time but poor frequency resolution, and vice versa (Gabor/uncertainty limit)
The number of FFT points only
Nothing; both can be made arbitrarily fine
items.explanation: The STFT uses one fixed window, so the time-frequency uncertainty (Gabor limit) forces a tradeoff: a short window localizes time well but smears frequency, while a long window sharpens frequency but blurs timing. Wavelets address this with multi-resolution analysis.

items.front: What is sampling, in one sentence?
items.back: Sampling is the process of converting a continuous-time signal x(t) into a discrete-time sequence x[n] by recording its amplitude at uniformly spaced instants n*Ts, where Ts is the sampling period and fs = 1/Ts is the sampling rate.
items.hint: Continuous time -> discrete time at regular intervals.

items.front: Mathematically, ideal (impulse) sampling is modeled as what operation on x(t)?
items.back: Multiplication of x(t) by a Dirac impulse train (Dirac comb) s(t) = sum over n of delta(t - n*Ts). The sampled signal is xs(t) = x(t) * s(t) = sum of x(n*Ts)*delta(t - n*Ts). This idealization captures the sample instants while ignoring real-world aperture/hold effects.
items.hint: Think of a comb of delta functions spaced Ts apart.

items.front: What does multiplication by an impulse train in the time domain do to the signal's spectrum?
items.back: It convolves the spectrum X(f) with an impulse train in frequency (spaced at fs). The result is periodic spectral replication: copies of X(f) appear centered at every integer multiple of fs (..., -fs, 0, +fs, +2*fs, ...), each scaled by 1/Ts.
items.hint: Time-domain multiplication = frequency-domain convolution; the comb spacing is fs.

items.front: State the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem.
items.back: A baseband signal containing no frequency components above B Hz is completely determined by, and can be exactly reconstructed from, samples taken at a rate fs > 2*B. Equivalently, sampling at strictly more than twice the highest frequency preserves all information.
items.hint: Strictly greater than twice the highest frequency.

items.front: Distinguish the Nyquist RATE from the Nyquist FREQUENCY.
items.back: Nyquist RATE = 2*B: the minimum SAMPLING rate needed to capture a signal of bandwidth/highest-frequency B (a property of the SIGNAL). Nyquist FREQUENCY = fs/2: the highest signal frequency that a given sample rate fs can represent without aliasing (a property of the SAMPLER). They are easy to confuse because both involve a factor of 2.
items.hint: Rate is 2*B (about the signal); frequency is fs/2 (about the sampler).

items.prompt: An audio signal has energy up to 20 kHz. What is the Nyquist rate?
items.explanation: The Nyquist rate is 2*B = 2 * 20 kHz = 40 kHz. This is why CD audio uses 44.1 kHz, leaving a small guard band above 40 kHz for the anti-alias filter to roll off.

items.prompt: A baseband signal is sampled at fs = 100 MHz. What is the Nyquist frequency?
items.explanation: The Nyquist frequency is fs/2 = 100/2 = 50 MHz. Any baseband signal content above 50 MHz will alias unless removed by an anti-alias filter before the ADC.

items.prompt: Why does the sampling theorem require fs STRICTLY greater than 2*B rather than fs equal to 2*B?
choices: At exactly 2*B, adjacent spectral replicas just touch at fs/2, leaving no room for a realizable filter and risking ambiguity for components exactly at B (e.g., a sine at B can be missed if sampled at its zero crossings)
Sampling hardware physically cannot run at exactly 2*B
The DFT is undefined when fs equals 2*B
items.explanation: At fs = 2*B the replicas meet edge-to-edge with zero guard band, so an ideal brick-wall filter would be required, and a tone exactly at B can be sampled at its zero crossings and lost. Real systems sample at fs > 2*B (oversample) to leave a transition band.

items.front: In an ADC/embedded data acquisition chain, where does sampling actually occur and what is fs set by?
items.back: Sampling occurs in the sample-and-hold (track-and-hold) circuit at the ADC front end; fs is set by the ADC conversion clock (e.g., a timer-triggered conversion or a dedicated sample clock). The chosen fs together with the analog input bandwidth determines whether aliasing happens.
items.hint: Sample-and-hold + conversion clock.

items.prompt: After ideal sampling at rate fs, what does the spectrum of the sampled signal look like?
choices: A single copy of X(f) centered at 0
The baseband X(f) plus identical scaled replicas centered at every multiple of fs
X(f) shifted up by fs/2 only
items.explanation: Sampling replicates the baseband spectrum periodically at intervals of fs (images at +/-fs, +/-2*fs, ...). Reconstruction works by isolating just the baseband copy with a lowpass filter; aliasing occurs when these replicas overlap.

items.front: What is aliasing?
items.back: Aliasing is the irreversible corruption that occurs when a signal contains energy above the Nyquist frequency (fs/2). The spectral replicas overlap, so high-frequency content 'folds' down and masquerades as a lower frequency that is indistinguishable from a genuine low-frequency component.
items.hint: High frequencies impersonating low ones because replicas overlap.

items.front: Give the folding formula for the apparent (alias) frequency of a tone f sampled at fs.
items.back: Compute f mod fs to bring it into [0, fs). If the result is <= fs/2, that is the alias. If it is > fs/2, the alias is fs - (f mod fs). The spectrum 'folds' about each multiple of fs/2, so apparent frequency bounces between 0 and fs/2.
items.hint: Reduce mod fs, then reflect about fs/2 if needed.

items.prompt: A 30 kHz tone is sampled at fs = 50 kHz. What apparent (alias) frequency appears in the digitized signal?
items.explanation: fs/2 = 25 kHz, and 30 kHz > 25 kHz so it aliases. 30 mod 50 = 30, which exceeds 25, so the alias = 50 - 30 = 20 kHz. The 30 kHz tone is indistinguishable from a real 20 kHz tone.

items.prompt: A 70 kHz tone is sampled at fs = 50 kHz. What is the alias frequency?
items.explanation: 70 mod 50 = 20 kHz, which is below fs/2 = 25 kHz, so the alias is 20 kHz directly (no further reflection needed). A tone one full fs above 20 kHz lands back at 20 kHz.

items.front: What is an anti-aliasing filter and where does it go?
items.back: An anti-aliasing filter is an analog lowpass filter placed BEFORE the ADC (sample-and-hold). It attenuates all energy above fs/2 (ideally above the band of interest) so that out-of-band signals and noise cannot fold into the passband. It must be analog because once sampled, aliasing is irreversible.
items.hint: Analog LPF before the sampler; must act before digitization.

items.front: Why does oversampling relax the anti-aliasing filter requirements?
items.back: Sampling well above 2*B widens the gap between the signal band edge (B) and the Nyquist frequency (fs/2). The filter's transition band can be much gentler (lower order, cheaper, less phase distortion) because anything that aliases must lie between B and fs - B, which is now far away. Oversampling trades data rate for analog filter simplicity.
items.hint: Bigger guard band = gentler filter roll-off.

items.prompt: A radar receiver digitizes a video/IF band of 0 to 5 MHz with an ADC at fs = 12 MHz. A strong out-of-band interferer at 8 MHz leaks past a weak anti-alias filter. Where does it appear?
choices: It is harmless because it is above the 5 MHz band
At 4 MHz, landing inside the 0 to 5 MHz band of interest
At 8 MHz, still out of band
At 12 MHz, removed by the next stage
items.explanation: fs/2 = 6 MHz; 8 MHz > 6 MHz so it aliases. 8 mod 12 = 8 > 6, so alias = 12 - 8 = 4 MHz, which falls inside the 0-5 MHz band and corrupts the data. This is exactly why anti-alias filtering matters in receiver design.

items.front: What is ideal reconstruction (sinc interpolation)?
items.back: Perfect reconstruction passes the sampled signal through an ideal lowpass (brick-wall) filter with cutoff fs/2. In the time domain this convolves each sample with a sinc pulse: x(t) = sum of x[n] * sinc((t - n*Ts)/Ts). The sincs sum to exactly reproduce the band-limited original. It is ideal but non-causal/infinite, so not realizable exactly.
items.hint: Brick-wall LPF in frequency = sinc interpolation in time.

items.prompt: A practical DAC outputs a zero-order hold (ZOH): each sample value is held constant for one period Ts. What spectral effect does ZOH introduce?
choices: A sinc-shaped (sin(pi*f/fs)/(pi*f/fs)) amplitude droop across the band plus residual images, usually corrected by a sinc/x-compensation filter
It removes all images automatically with no droop
items.explanation: ZOH convolves with a rectangular pulse of width Ts, whose transform is a sinc. This causes passband amplitude droop (about -3.9 dB at fs/2) and only partially suppresses images, so DAC chains use a sinc-compensation (inverse-sinc) filter plus an analog reconstruction lowpass.

items.prompt: Which statement best explains why aliasing cannot be undone in software after the ADC?
choices: Once overlapping replicas are summed during sampling, the aliased and genuine components occupy the same bins and are mathematically inseparable
Digital filters only work on real signals
items.explanation: Aliasing adds the folded high-frequency energy directly onto legitimate in-band content. After summation there is no information left to separate them, so prevention (analog anti-alias filtering or adequate fs) is the only cure.

items.front: What is quantization, and how many levels does an N-bit ADC provide?
items.back: Quantization maps the continuous sampled amplitude to the nearest of a finite set of discrete codes. An N-bit ADC has 2^N levels with step size (LSB) q = full-scale-range / 2^N. The rounding error introduced is quantization error, bounded by +/- q/2.
items.hint: 2^N levels; step q = FSR / 2^N.

items.front: What is quantization noise and what is its typical model?
items.back: Quantization noise is the error signal between the true and quantized values. For a busy signal spanning many codes it is modeled as additive noise uniformly distributed on [-q/2, +q/2], approximately white, with variance q^2/12 (RMS = q/sqrt(12)). This model underpins the SNR-vs-bits formula.
items.hint: Uniform on +/- q/2; variance q^2/12.

items.front: State the ideal ADC SNR formula and the '6 dB per bit' rule of thumb.
items.back: For a full-scale sine into an ideal N-bit ADC: SNR = 6.02*N + 1.76 dB. So each added bit improves SNR by about 6.02 dB. The 1.76 dB term comes from the sine's crest factor relative to the uniform-noise model.

items.prompt: Using SNR = 6.02*N + 1.76 dB, what is the ideal SNR of a 12-bit ADC for a full-scale sine?
items.explanation: 6.02 * 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 dB. Each bit adds about 6 dB, so a 16-bit ADC would reach about 98.1 dB.

items.prompt: A radar receiver must capture a 78 dB instantaneous dynamic range. Ignoring processing gain and oversampling, what is the minimum ADC bit depth from the 6.02*N + 1.76 dB rule?
items.explanation: Solve 6.02*N + 1.76 >= 78 -> N >= 76.24/6.02 = 12.66, so N = 13 bits gives 6.02*13 + 1.76 = 80.0 dB. 12 bits (74 dB) is insufficient; this is the core 'justify bit depth from SNR' calculation.

items.front: What is dither and why deliberately add noise before an ADC?
items.back: Dither is a small amount of noise added before quantization to decorrelate the quantization error from the signal. It breaks up harmonic/pattern distortion (turning it into a smoother noise floor) and, combined with averaging/oversampling, can recover sub-LSB resolution. Useful for low-level signals near the LSB.
items.hint: Add noise to randomize quantization error and kill spurs.

items.prompt: Processing gain: oversampling by a factor OSR and then digitally filtering to bandwidth B spreads quantization noise over fs/2 but keeps signal in B. Doubling fs (OSR x2) over the same band improves SNR by approximately how much?
items.explanation: Quantization noise is spread roughly white over 0 to fs/2; only the fraction B/(fs/2) remains after decimation/filtering. Each 2x in fs halves the in-band noise power = +3 dB SNR (equivalent to about 0.5 extra bit, more with noise-shaping sigma-delta).

items.front: What is bandpass (under)sampling and why is it used in SDR/radar?
items.back: Bandpass sampling deliberately samples an RF signal of bandwidth B centered at high carrier fc at a rate fs much less than 2*fc (but still > 2*B). The signal lies in a higher Nyquist zone; aliasing is used ON PURPOSE to fold the RF band down to baseband/low-IF. This eliminates an analog downconversion stage in SDR/radar receivers.
items.hint: Sample faster than 2*B but slower than 2*fc; use aliasing intentionally.

items.prompt: For bandpass sampling, the key constraint (besides fs > 2*B) that must hold to avoid the band straddling a multiple of fs/2 is:
choices: The entire band must fit within a single Nyquist zone, i.e., a valid fs satisfies 2*fc_high/n <= fs <= 2*fc_low/(n-1) for some integer n with n <= fc_high/B
B must be a power of two
items.explanation: The band must land entirely inside one Nyquist zone (no fold across a k*fs/2 boundary). Valid rates fall in bands given by 2*fc_high/n <= fs <= 2*fc_low/(n-1), where the zone index n is bounded by floor(fc_high/B). Picking fs from a valid band folds the RF cleanly to baseband.

items.prompt: A signal occupies 200 to 220 MHz (B = 20 MHz, fc = 210 MHz). A receiver bandpass-samples it at fs = 80 MHz. To which baseband frequency range does the 200-220 MHz band fold?
items.explanation: Reduce edges mod fs=80: 200 mod 80 = 40, 220 mod 80 = 60. Both fall in the 2nd half of a Nyquist zone (40-80 with fs/2=40... here zone 3: 160-200 is up-going, 200-240 is down-going), so the band reflects to 80-60=20 and 80-40=40, i.e. 20 to 40 MHz (spectrum inverted). It fits cleanly in one zone since fs=80 > 2*B=40.

items.front: What is I/Q sampling (complex baseband) and why is it used?
items.back: I/Q sampling represents a signal as a complex stream I + jQ, where I (in-phase) and Q (quadrature) are the outputs of mixing with cos and sin of the carrier (90 degrees apart). Complex baseband captures both positive and negative frequencies independently, allows asymmetric spectra, doubles usable bandwidth per real sample rate, and is the native format of SDR/radar receivers.
items.hint: Two channels 90 degrees apart -> complex stream that distinguishes +f from -f.

items.prompt: For a COMPLEX (I/Q) baseband signal of one-sided bandwidth B (occupying -B/2 to +B/2), the minimum complex sample rate to avoid aliasing is:
items.explanation: A complex signal spanning total bandwidth B needs only fs = B complex samples/s (each complex sample is two real numbers, so the real-equivalent rate still meets Nyquist). This is why SDRs quote complex sample rate equal to the channel bandwidth, not twice it.

items.front: Define decimation and interpolation in a multirate DSP chain.
items.back: Decimation reduces the sample rate by integer M: first lowpass-filter to fs/(2*M) to prevent aliasing, then keep every Mth sample (downsample). Interpolation increases the rate by integer L: insert L-1 zeros between samples (upsample), then lowpass-filter to remove the resulting images. Together (L/M) they give rational rate conversion.
items.hint: Decimate = filter then drop samples; interpolate = zero-stuff then filter.

items.prompt: When decimating by M, why must the lowpass (decimation) filter come BEFORE discarding samples?
choices: Because dropping samples lowers the effective fs to fs/M, so any content above fs/(2*M) would alias unless removed first
Filters only work at high rates
It does not matter; order is arbitrary
items.explanation: Downsampling by M reduces the Nyquist frequency from fs/2 to fs/(2*M). Energy between the new and old Nyquist edges must be filtered out first, otherwise it folds into the retained band. (Polyphase implementations fold the filter and the downsampler together efficiently, but logically the filtering still precedes the rate drop.)

items.front: State the three Kolmogorov axioms of probability.
items.back: 1) Non-negativity: P(A) >= 0 for any event A. 2) Normalization: P(S) = 1 for the sample space S. 3) Countable additivity: for mutually exclusive events A1, A2, ..., P(union of Ai) = sum of P(Ai).
items.hint: Non-negative, total mass 1, additivity over disjoint events.

items.front: Define conditional probability P(A|B) and state when it is defined.
items.back: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B), defined only when P(B) > 0. It rescales the probability of A to the reduced sample space in which B is known to have occurred.
items.hint: Joint over the conditioning event's probability.

items.front: When are two events A and B independent? Give both the product form and the conditional form.
items.back: Product form: P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B). Equivalent conditional form (when P(B) > 0): P(A|B) = P(A). Independence means knowing B occurred does not change the probability of A.
items.hint: Joint factors into the product of marginals.

items.front: State Bayes' theorem and name each part: posterior, likelihood, prior, evidence.
items.back: P(H|D) = P(D|H) * P(H) / P(D). P(H|D) is the posterior, P(D|H) is the likelihood, P(H) is the prior, and P(D) is the evidence (marginal likelihood), often expanded via the law of total probability as P(D) = sum over h of P(D|h)P(h).
items.hint: Posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior.

items.front: In radar detection, what do Pd (probability of detection) and Pfa (probability of false alarm) mean in conditional-probability terms?
items.back: Pd = P(declare target | target present) is the conditional probability the detector fires given a real target. Pfa = P(declare target | no target present) is the conditional probability the detector fires on noise alone. They are the radar names for the true-positive rate and false-positive rate.
items.hint: Both are conditioned on ground truth (target present vs absent).

items.prompt: A radar has Pd = 0.9 and Pfa = 0.01. The prior probability a target is actually present in a given cell is P(target) = 0.001. Given a detection (alarm), what is the approximate posterior probability a target is truly present, P(target | alarm)?
items.explanation: By Bayes: P(target|alarm) = (0.9*0.001) / (0.9*0.001 + 0.01*0.999) = 0.0009 / (0.0009 + 0.00999) = 0.0009 / 0.01089 ~= 0.083. Despite high Pd, the rare prior plus nonzero Pfa makes most alarms false. This is the base-rate effect.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes 'mutually exclusive' from 'independent' events?
choices: They mean the same thing for any two events
Mutually exclusive events with nonzero probability are always independent
Mutually exclusive events with nonzero probability are actually dependent, since one occurring forces the other to not occur
items.explanation: If A and B are mutually exclusive then P(A and B) = 0, but P(A)P(B) > 0 when both have nonzero probability, so they cannot be independent. Knowing A occurred tells you B did not, which is strong dependence.

items.prompt: The law of total probability expresses P(D) for a partition {H1,...,Hn} of the sample space as:
choices: P(D) = product over i of P(D|Hi)
P(D) = sum over i of P(D|Hi) * P(Hi)
P(D) = max over i of P(D|Hi)
items.explanation: P(D) = sum_i P(D and Hi) = sum_i P(D|Hi)P(Hi). This is exactly the denominator (evidence) in Bayes' theorem and how you marginalize out the hypothesis.

items.front: What is the complement rule and how does it help compute 'at least one' events?
items.back: P(not A) = 1 - P(A). For 'at least one success' in independent trials it is usually easier to compute P(none) first: P(at least one) = 1 - P(no successes) = 1 - product of (1 - p_i).
items.hint: Subtract the probability of the easy opposite event from 1.

items.prompt: Three events A, B, C are pairwise independent but NOT mutually independent. Which equality can FAIL?
choices: P(A and B and C) = P(A)P(B)P(C)
items.explanation: Pairwise independence guarantees all the two-event products factor, but mutual independence additionally requires the three-way joint to factor. The triple product can fail even when every pair is independent.

lessons.title: Random Variables: PMF, PDF, CDF, Moments

items.front: Distinguish a PMF from a PDF, and state what each must satisfy.
items.back: A PMF p(x) applies to discrete random variables: p(x) = P(X = x), with 0 <= p(x) <= 1 and sum over x of p(x) = 1. A PDF f(x) applies to continuous random variables: f(x) >= 0 and the integral over all x of f(x) dx = 1. For a PDF, f(x) is a density, not a probability, so f(x) can exceed 1; only integrals over intervals give probabilities.
items.hint: Discrete sums to 1; continuous integrates to 1.

items.front: Define the CDF F(x) and list its key properties.
items.back: F(x) = P(X <= x). Properties: non-decreasing, right-continuous, F(-inf) = 0, F(+inf) = 1. For continuous X, the PDF is f(x) = dF/dx; for discrete X the CDF is a step function. P(a < X <= b) = F(b) - F(a).
items.hint: Accumulated probability up to x; derivative gives the density.

items.front: Define expectation E[X] for discrete and continuous random variables.
items.back: Discrete: E[X] = sum over x of x * p(x). Continuous: E[X] = integral of x * f(x) dx over all x. Expectation is linear: E[aX + bY + c] = a E[X] + b E[Y] + c, regardless of dependence.
items.hint: Probability-weighted average of the values.

items.front: Define variance Var(X) and give the shortcut formula in terms of moments.
items.back: Var(X) = E[(X - E[X])^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2. It is the second central moment, measuring spread. Standard deviation is sigma = sqrt(Var(X)). For a constant a: Var(aX) = a^2 Var(X).
items.hint: Mean of the square minus the square of the mean.

items.front: What are the first four moments and what does each describe intuitively?
items.back: 1st (mean) = location/center. 2nd central (variance) = spread. 3rd standardized (skewness) = asymmetry. 4th standardized (kurtosis) = tail heaviness / peakedness. Moments are E[X^n]; central moments are E[(X - mu)^n].
items.hint: Center, spread, asymmetry, tails.

items.prompt: For a continuous random variable X with PDF f(x), what is P(X = a) for any single point a?
items.explanation: For a continuous distribution, the probability of any exact value is zero because it is the integral of the density over a zero-width interval. Only intervals carry positive probability; f(a) is a density, not P(X=a).

items.prompt: Thermal (Johnson) noise voltage is often modeled as zero-mean. If the noise has mean 0 and variance sigma^2, what is E[X^2], the mean-square value (related to noise power)?
items.explanation: Var(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2. With E[X] = 0, E[X^2] = Var(X) = sigma^2. The mean-square value equals the variance for a zero-mean signal, which is why variance maps directly to noise power.

items.prompt: Which is the correct relationship between the PDF and CDF of a continuous random variable?
choices: f(x) = dF(x)/dx and F(x) = integral of f(t) dt from -inf to x
items.explanation: The PDF is the derivative of the CDF, and the CDF is the integral of the PDF from -infinity up to x. They are an integral/derivative pair.

items.prompt: A fair die is rolled. What is E[X] and Var(X) for the outcome X in {1,...,6}?

items.front: What is the Law of the Unconscious Statistician (LOTUS)?
items.back: To find the expected value of a function g(X), you do NOT need the distribution of g(X). Discrete: E[g(X)] = sum of g(x) p(x). Continuous: E[g(X)] = integral of g(x) f(x) dx. This is how you compute things like E[X^2] or E[exp(X)] directly from the distribution of X.
items.hint: Apply g to the values, weight by the distribution of X.

items.prompt: If Y = aX + b, how do the mean and variance transform?
choices: E[Y] = aE[X] + b, Var(Y) = a^2 Var(X)
E[Y] = aE[X] + b, Var(Y) = a Var(X) + b
E[Y] = aE[X], Var(Y) = a^2 Var(X) + b^2
items.explanation: Expectation is linear so E[aX+b] = aE[X]+b. Adding a constant b shifts the distribution but does not change spread, and scaling by a multiplies variance by a^2: Var(aX+b) = a^2 Var(X).

lessons.title: Key Distributions, CLT, and Radar Statistics

items.front: Give the mean and variance of: Bernoulli(p), Binomial(n,p), and Poisson(lambda).
items.back: Bernoulli(p): mean p, variance p(1-p). Binomial(n,p): mean np, variance np(1-p) (sum of n independent Bernoulli). Poisson(lambda): mean lambda, variance lambda (mean equals variance). Poisson is the limit of Binomial as n grows large and p shrinks with np -> lambda.
items.hint: Poisson is special: mean = variance = lambda.

items.front: Write the Gaussian (normal) PDF N(mu, sigma^2) and state the 68-95-99.7 rule.
items.back: f(x) = (1 / (sigma * sqrt(2*pi))) * exp(-(x - mu)^2 / (2*sigma^2)). Empirical rule: about 68% of mass within mu +/- 1 sigma, 95% within mu +/- 2 sigma, 99.7% within mu +/- 3 sigma.
items.hint: Bell curve; standardize with z = (x - mu)/sigma.

items.front: What is the exponential distribution used for, and what is its key memoryless property?
items.back: Exponential(lambda) models waiting time between events in a Poisson process; PDF f(x) = lambda*exp(-lambda*x) for x >= 0, mean 1/lambda, variance 1/lambda^2. Memoryless: P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t). The remaining wait does not depend on how long you have already waited.
items.hint: Waiting times; future is independent of elapsed time.

items.front: Why do Rayleigh and Rician distributions appear in radar and fading channels?
items.back: When in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) components are independent zero-mean Gaussians (noise only), the envelope magnitude sqrt(I^2 + Q^2) is Rayleigh-distributed. When there is also a deterministic (line-of-sight or target) component, the envelope is Rician. Rayleigh = noise-only / non-line-of-sight fading; Rician = signal-plus-noise with a dominant component. The Rician K-factor is the ratio of dominant power to scattered power.
items.hint: Envelope of complex Gaussian: zero-mean -> Rayleigh, nonzero mean -> Rician.

items.front: How does the chi-squared distribution arise in radar detection and statistics?
items.back: Chi-squared with k degrees of freedom is the distribution of the sum of squares of k independent standard normals. Squaring a Rayleigh envelope (I^2 + Q^2) gives an exponential, which is chi-squared with 2 degrees of freedom; this models the power of noise or a fluctuating (Swerling) target. Chi-squared also underlies variance estimation, goodness-of-fit tests, and confidence intervals for variance.
items.hint: Sum of squared Gaussians; envelope power is chi-squared with 2 dof.

items.front: State the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) and why it matters for noise.
items.back: The sum (or mean) of a large number of independent, identically distributed random variables with finite mean mu and variance sigma^2 tends toward a Gaussian: the sample mean approaches N(mu, sigma^2/n). This is why thermal noise, the aggregate of countless tiny random electron motions, is well modeled as Gaussian regardless of the microscopic details.
items.hint: Sums of many independent effects look Gaussian.

items.prompt: A radar receiver outputs in-phase I and quadrature Q noise samples, each independent N(0, sigma^2). The detector forms the envelope R = sqrt(I^2 + Q^2). What is the distribution of R?
items.explanation: The magnitude of a zero-mean complex Gaussian (independent zero-mean I and Q with equal variance) is Rayleigh-distributed. If a deterministic target component were added (nonzero mean), it would become Rician instead.
